text: Seneca was fascinated — and troubled — by the Roman salutatio, the morning ritual in which clients lined up at a patron's door to pay respects. The great man would emerge, receive their greetings, and dispense small favors. Entire mornings dissolved in this theater of obligation. Seneca saw it as civilized robbery: both parties surrendered their best hours to a performance neither enjoyed. In Letter I he counsels Lucilius to shrink his obligations ruthlessly. The Stoic goal is not rudeness but what Seneca calls negotium sibi — business with oneself. Every hour given to hollow ceremony is an hour stolen from becoming who you could be.
question: What does Seneca call the Stoic goal of reducing empty obligations?

options.text: Ante nos — looking forward from life's end
Vindicatio temporis — legal reclamation of time
